steps.title: Spot the causal link
steps.content:
The passage provides two key pieces: (1) nestlings show an early preference for their own local dialect, and (2) “song preference plays a role in songbird mate selection.” This sets up a causal inference: if young birds develop a preference for local songs and song preference influences who they choose to mate with, then that early preference should predict their future reproductive choices.

The blank must complete this chain.

steps.title: Match the causal chain to the choices
steps.content:
Choice C directly connects the early preference to future behavior: the nestlings' early dialect preference would “drive them when they mature to reproduce with other F. hypoleuca from local rather than nonlocal populations.” This is the logical consequence of the evidence—early preference → future mate selection.

A reverses the prediction (the preference disappears, which contradicts why the early finding matters), B invents an unsupported claim about food rewards not mentioned in the study, and D extends the finding to other songbird species that were never tested.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
